deck: Mercy Corps' MEL team on moving from fragmented monitoring and evaluation to a connected digital ecosystem, and how standardizing tools across 40 countries fuels program adaptation and faster, more comprehensive frontline impact.
seo_description: Mercy Corps' MEL team on building a connected digital ecosystem across 40 countries to turn monitoring and evaluation data into faster frontline impact.
og_description: How Mercy Corps' MEL team standardized tools across 40 countries to turn data into faster, comprehensive humanitarian impact.
about: Jonathan Jackson sits down with Mercy Corps' Monitoring, Evaluation and Learning (MEL) team: Meri Ghorkhmazyan, Senior Director MEL, Alex Tran, Senior MEL Advisor, and Hanna Camp, Senior Advisor for Technology for MEL. Together they unpack how MEL is the next generation of M&E, adding an explicit learning element that connects data to program design and decision making rather than just accountability reporting.

The team describes the long change management journey Mercy Corps took to fix a deeply disaggregated approach to MEL technology, where country offices ran as many as five to seven overlapping data collection tools with little global guidance or support. Through surveys, interviews, a white paper, and a dedicated task force, they built a recommended suite of private and open source tools, secured enterprise agreements including one for CommCare, and launched training and Jumpstart funds to support adoption.

The conversation also looks ahead to aggregating standardized data across humanitarian responses to surface country, regional, and global trends, and returns again and again to a central lesson: many struggles with technology are really culture, resource, and buy-in issues, so lasting change demands a comprehensive approach, not just better tools.

pull_quote.text: Along the way, we found out that sometimes our struggles with technology have nothing to do with the technology. These are culture issues. These are resource issues. These are buy-in issues.

bullets: MEL vs. M&E: why adding learning turns data from accountability reporting into fuel for program adaptation
Timeliness and comprehensiveness: how an integrated tech stack cut one team's process from three weeks to as little as one day
From disaggregated to standardized: country offices once ran five to seven overlapping data collection tools with no global support
The white paper and task force Mercy Corps used to build buy-in and action a recommended suite of MEL technologies
Enterprise agreements: how going enterprise with CommCare saved money, time, and procurement headaches
Talent and technology go hand in hand, with standardized tools opening clearer professional growth pathways
What comes next: aggregating standardized data and pairing it with secondary sources like earth observation to see deeper trends

stats.label: data turnaround reduced by moving from a manual to a technology-driven process

guests.name: Meri Ghorkhmazyan
guests.role: Senior Director MEL, setting organization-wide priorities for monitoring, evaluation and learning and leading the change management effort.

guests.name: Alex Tran
guests.role: Senior MEL Advisor focused on integrating technologies to automate data and enhance decision making in humanitarian response programs.

guests.name: Hanna Camp
guests.role: Senior Advisor for Technology for MEL, leading work to standardize and support MEL technology across offices and programs.
